title: Mornings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: Houghton enacts a one-year data center ban amid electricity shortages and resident pushback.

lines.text: Good morning and welcome to Mornings with Graham & Emmy — the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
Today we're diving into Houghton's brand-new one-year moratorium on data centers, a move driven by residents worried about power and water.
That's a big step for the Upper Peninsula, Graham. Why now, and what does it really change for the region?

The city council voted yesterday to hit pause while they draft rules on power use, noise, and water. Houghton already struggles with electricity supply, so folks want time to figure out if these massive facilities belong there.
How did we get here? Has this kind of pushback been building for a while?
Resident concerns have been growing as proposals pop up. People are looking at the strain on the local grid and asking whether one big project could tie up transmission capacity for years.
That makes sense. What exactly are the stakes for electric bills if a large data center moves in?
Data centers are huge power users. In a place already short on electricity, adding one could mean higher rates or even reliability issues for everyone else on the system.
And water is another big piece, right? These facilities need a lot for cooling.
Exactly. The moratorium gives the city breathing room to write protections around water use too, so residents aren't left guessing about long-term supply.
What about farmland and smaller towns nearby? Does this ripple out?
It already is. Neighboring townships are now looking at their own pauses after seeing Houghton's action, according to the Mining Journal.
So it's moving from one city to a broader conversation about who controls this kind of growth.
Right. The debate has shifted to a multi-township level, with residents asking how much local say they keep when these projects arrive.
Who are the main players pushing for the rules versus those wanting the projects?

On one side you have city council and residents focused on protecting existing infrastructure and quality of life. On the other, developers see economic potential, though the pause puts that on hold for now.
What happens next in Houghton itself?
They'll spend the year writing those specific rules on power, noise, and water. Similar discussions are already starting in nearby areas.
And if other townships follow suit, how might that affect the bigger picture across the UP?
It could slow the pace of new builds while communities decide what fits their capacity. That gives everyone more time to weigh the trade-offs.
Sounds like a pattern we're seeing elsewhere too. Let's move to some quick hits from around the state.
Starting with Portage, where new zoning language would keep large data centers out of most residential and agricultural zones.
Kalamazoo residents have been complaining about noise, traffic, and grid strain. This comes after months of concerns about industrial creep into rural edges.
Next, a rural township delayed its vote on a major data-center tax deal after dozens of residents showed up to object.
The project would use lots of electricity and water but deliver limited local tax revenue for years. Homeowners are worried about possible rate hikes.
In another community, residents filed a federal lawsuit claiming nonstop noise from an AI data center has made normal life impossible.
They're asking a judge to step in after local officials didn't act. That could set a precedent if it moves forward.
Eaton County commissioners questioned an Amazon-linked data center project and told staff to draft the county's first zoning rules for the use.
Nearby residents could face higher utility bills and strained water if it proceeds. This is the first time the county has moved on formal rules.
In Saline Township, the board postponed action on a 50% tax abatement for an Oracle data center after residents raised concerns over water, noise, and local costs for the big project.
That 12-year break is now on hold while they sort through those issues.
Girard Township approved strict new rules including setbacks, noise limits, and height caps that make large projects much harder to build.
Branch County officials say smaller facilities can still go forward if they meet the standards, giving residents near Coldwater clearer protections.
A proposal near Mason could bypass township review and go straight to state regulators, which has Ingham County residents worried about losing input on power use and tax deals.
That raises bigger questions about local control over these industrial-scale projects.
Finally, York Township scheduled a hearing on its own data center moratorium after similar pauses passed in nearby Adrian and other towns.
Washtenaw County is seeing rising opposition, and residents want time to assess impacts on roads, water, and electricity rates.
Let's look ahead to what's coming up that listeners can actually attend.
Today the Michigan Public Service Commission has an open meeting that covers large-load electric agreements.
That's directly tied to how these data centers connect to the grid and what agreements get approved.
Then on Monday the 13th, Lansing City Council Development & Planning holds a public hearing on a proposed 182-day data center moratorium.
Anyone in the area can weigh in there on how the city should handle future proposals.
Plenty of chances for residents to stay involved.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker. We're back tomorrow.

episode_title: Houghton Pauses Data Centers for a Year as Power Concerns Grow
